---
title: "Day 2 — Exercise: Quality trimming as a slice view"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write `quality_trim(qual: &[u8], min_score: u8) -> &[u8]`: given a FASTQ quality string (Phred+33 encoded — see [day 1 exercise 4](../day1/04-phred.qmd)) and a minimum quality **score**, return the largest interior slice of `qual` whose endpoints both pass the threshold.

That is: trim low-quality bases from the start and the end, but leave the middle alone.

The function returns a [`&[u8]`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html) — a view into the input — not a new `Vec<u8>`. No bytes are copied.

## Background

Per-end quality trimming is the simplest quality-control step in any sequencing pipeline. (Real tools like `cutadapt` or `fastp` use sliding-window averages; we will stay simpler for today.) Conventionally:

1. Move the start index forward as long as the leading base is below threshold.
2. Move the end index backward as long as the trailing base is below threshold.
3. Return the slice `&qual[start..end]`.

The input quality is stored as Phred+33 bytes (so `'!'` = score 0, `'I'` = score 40); the threshold is given as a score. Convert the score to a byte threshold by adding 33.

## Why a slice, not a `Vec`?

Returning `&[u8]` means the function performs **zero allocation**. The returned slice points into the same memory as `qual`. The caller can use it for as long as `qual` itself is alive — the borrow checker tracks this for you, with no annotations needed (single input reference → its lifetime is the output's).

## The exercise

```bash
cd day2/ex-quality-trim
```

Make all seven tests pass.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
# A FASTQ quality string of "!!IIII!!" trimmed at score 30:
# bytes:  ! ! I I I I ! !
# scores: 0 0 40 40 40 40 0 0
# trimmed (drop leading and trailing low):  I I I I
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — convert score to byte threshold"}
The quality bytes are Phred+33-encoded, so a score *q* is stored as the byte *q* + 33. Compute the byte threshold once at the start of the function:

```rust
let threshold = min_score + 33;
```

After this, every comparison is just `qual[i] >= threshold`.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — advance the start"}
Walk the start index forward while the leading byte fails the threshold. Use a [`while` loop](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch03-05-control-flow.html#conditional-loops-with-while):

```rust
let mut start: usize = 0;
while start < qual.len() && qual[start] < threshold {
    start += 1;
}
```

The `start < qual.len()` check is essential — without it, indexing past the end of the slice would panic. The [short-circuit `&&`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/operator-expr.html#lazy-boolean-operators) guarantees `qual[start]` is only evaluated when `start` is in range.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — pull the end back"}
Symmetrically, walk the end index backward while the trailing byte fails:

```rust
let mut end: usize = qual.len();
while end > start && qual[end - 1] < threshold {
    end -= 1;
}
```

The `end > start` guard is important: if **every** byte is below threshold, this loop should leave `end == start`, returning an empty slice. (Without the guard the loop would underflow `end` and panic.)
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — return the view"}
The final expression is the slice:

```rust
&qual[start..end]
```

No `;`, no allocation — just a view.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn quality_trim(qual: &[u8], min_score: u8) -> &[u8] {
    let threshold = min_score + 33;
    let mut start: usize = 0;
    while start < qual.len() && qual[start] < threshold {
        start += 1;
    }
    let mut end: usize = qual.len();
    while end > start && qual[end - 1] < threshold {
        end -= 1;
    }
    &qual[start..end]
}
```

Full reference in `day2/ex-quality-trim-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Day 3**: rewrite with [`<[T]>::iter().position()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.Iterator.html#method.position) and [`rposition()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.DoubleEndedIterator.html#method.rposition) — same algorithm, no explicit loop, no mutable counters.
- **Day 4**: apply this to every read of a real `.fastq.gz` streamed through [`noodles-fastq`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/), then write the trimmed reads to a new file.
